title: Business Model Resilience Stress Test

introduction.subtitle: Risk and resilience assessment. 7-9 min.
introduction.description:
Business Model Resilience Stress Test helps you learn the vocabulary and typical expectations behind Demand Concentration, Margin Flexibility, Cash Shock Capacity, Operational Dependencies. Use it as a private diagnostic: answer from observable evidence, not aspiration, and treat the result as a map for the next improvement cycle.

This educational quiz runs entirely in your browser. You receive the baseline result without account creation or email.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is explicit and share-safe.
introduction.disclaimer: Educational diagnostic only. Not legal, tax, financial, investment, medical, employment, regulatory, or psychometric certification advice.

questions.text: How dependent is revenue on the largest customer or customer group?

options.text: A. There is no usable model; the answer is unknown or based on rough intuition.
B. There is an estimate, but assumptions, sensitivity, and ownership are weak.
C. The model uses current inputs, documented assumptions, and regular review.
D. The model is scenario-tested, tied to triggers and decisions, and reconciled with reliable source data.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Demand Concentration: How dependent is revenue on the largest customer or customer group
conceptHelp.summary: Demand Concentration means how exposed the business is to one customer, segment, channel, or demand source and how that exposure changes under stress. This question narrows the concept to "How dependent is revenue on the largest customer or customer group?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Demand Concentration matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show the current model behind "How dependent is revenue on the largest customer or customer group?", the assumptions behind it, the owner, and the decision that changes when the numbers move.

questions.text: How dependent is growth on a single acquisition or distribution channel?

options.text: A. The answer is broad, assumed, or based mostly on internal belief.
B. There is a high-level description, but segmentation, trigger, or buyer evidence is thin.
C. The answer is defined for a specific segment with documented behaviour, alternatives, and decision criteria.
D. The answer is validated across repeated cases and directly informs targeting, messaging, prioritisation, and trade-offs.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How well can gross margin absorb supplier, infrastructure, or service-cost increases?

options.text: A. There is no usable model; the answer is unknown or based on rough intuition.
B. There is an estimate, but assumptions, sensitivity, and ownership are weak.
C. The model uses current inputs, documented assumptions, and regular review.
D. The model is scenario-tested, tied to triggers and decisions, and reconciled with reliable source data.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Margin Flexibility: How well can gross margin absorb supplier, infrastructure, or service-cost increases
conceptHelp.summary: Margin Flexibility means whether gross margin and operating costs can absorb shocks without destroying the business model. This question narrows the concept to "How well can gross margin absorb supplier, infrastructure, or service-cost increases?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Margin Flexibility matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show the current model behind "How well can gross margin absorb supplier, infrastructure, or service-cost increases?", the assumptions behind it, the owner, and the decision that changes when the numbers move.

questions.text: How flexible is the operating cost base during a downturn?

options.text: A. This is not explicit yet in Margin Flexibility; decisions depend on intuition, habit, or one-off judgement.
B. There is a plausible practice in Margin Flexibility, but evidence, ownership, or repeatability is still partial.
C. Margin Flexibility has a documented practice with examples, owners, and current evidence used in decisions.
D. Margin Flexibility is reviewed, measured over time, and strong enough to guide trade-offs under pressure.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: What cash buffer exists under a realistic downside case?

options.text: A. There is no usable model; the answer is unknown or based on rough intuition.
B. There is an estimate, but assumptions, sensitivity, and ownership are weak.
C. The model uses current inputs, documented assumptions, and regular review.
D. The model is scenario-tested, tied to triggers and decisions, and reconciled with reliable source data.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Cash Shock Capacity: What cash buffer exists under a realistic downside case
conceptHelp.summary: Cash Shock Capacity means how long the company can keep operating under realistic downside scenarios and whether actions are ready before cash pressure arrives. This question narrows the concept to "What cash buffer exists under a realistic downside case?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Cash Shock Capacity matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show the current model behind "What cash buffer exists under a realistic downside case?", the assumptions behind it, the owner, and the decision that changes when the numbers move.

options.text: A. This is not explicit yet in Cash Shock Capacity; decisions depend on intuition, habit, or one-off judgement.
B. There is a plausible practice in Cash Shock Capacity, but evidence, ownership, or repeatability is still partial.
C. Cash Shock Capacity has a documented practice with examples, owners, and current evidence used in decisions.
D. Cash Shock Capacity is reviewed, measured over time, and strong enough to guide trade-offs under pressure.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How dependent are critical operations on one person, supplier, or technology component?

options.text: A. The information is fragmented, missing, or too inconsistent to support decisions.
B. Partial tools or reports exist, but definitions and ownership are weak.
C. Definitions, owners, access rules, and regular review are governed.
D. Lineage, quality controls, exception handling, and a feedback loop into decisions exist.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Operational Dependencies: How dependent are critical operations on one person, supplier, or technology component
conceptHelp.summary: Operational Dependencies means single points of failure in people, suppliers, systems, processes, or infrastructure that could interrupt delivery. This question narrows the concept to "How dependent are critical operations on one person, supplier, or technology component?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Operational Dependencies matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show the current model behind "How dependent are critical operations on one person, supplier, or technology component?", the assumptions behind it, the owner, and the decision that changes when the numbers move.

questions.text: How rapidly could the business restore service after a major disruption?

options.text: A. It happens rarely, reactively, or only when a crisis forces attention.
B. It happens occasionally, but timing depends on individual initiative or urgency.
C. There is a defined cadence with clear participants, owners, and triggers.
D. The cadence is frequent enough to change decisions, includes quality checks, and improves from outcomes.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Operational Dependencies: How rapidly could the business restore service after a major disruption
conceptHelp.summary: Operational Dependencies means single points of failure in people, suppliers, systems, processes, or infrastructure that could interrupt delivery. This question narrows the concept to "How rapidly could the business restore service after a major disruption?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Operational Dependencies matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show the current model behind "How rapidly could the business restore service after a major disruption?", the assumptions behind it, the owner, and the decision that changes when the numbers move.

questions.text: How quickly would the business detect a dangerous concentration shift?

options.text: A. No concrete example shows demand concentration monitoring; the answer relies on intention, memory, or individual judgement.
B. There are isolated examples of demand concentration monitoring, but they are inconsistent, hard to verify, or depend on one person.
C. Recent examples show demand concentration monitoring in practice, with enough detail to judge quality and repeatability.
D. Demand concentration monitoring is repeatedly demonstrated under pressure and changes decisions, ownership, or trade-offs.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Demand Concentration: How quickly would the business detect a dangerous concentration shift
conceptHelp.summary: Demand Concentration is tested here through a concrete operating question: "How quickly would the business detect a dangerous concentration shift?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Demand Concentration matters because weak practice in this area can make the whole result look more mature than the operating system really is.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable behaviour: artifacts, decisions, customer evidence, owners, reviews, or shipped work. Do not score intention as practice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show current evidence, a named owner, and one recent decision changed by this practice.

questionStageTitle: Learn the concept, then answer from evidence
questionStageDescription: Use the concept button when a term is unfamiliar. Prefer observable evidence over aspiration or ideal self-description.
